text:
In the forest glade stood an ancient oak, whose leaves had never fallen. A shadow passed by, and from its branches fell a feather. A bird fluttered away, humming with delight. It would sit on the tree and pluck a feather at dawn every day until winter.

Children heard stories of the feathering tree from faraway lands. They would sneak into the forest to catch glimpses of the joyful bird. The stories inspired dreams of flights across the ocean. Even foxes sang ballads about the featherer bird.

One autumn, a brave squirrel gathered a dozen feathers. It brought them back to its home on a mossy stump. Every night, the squirrel pelted tiny knock-knock sounds onto the leaves. One night, the feather tree was awakened by the persistent tapping. The bird fluttered down, took the feathers from the squirrel, and hummed happily. "Keep bringing them," the bird said. And so the squirrel did. For decades.

The trees flour
